review_summary: Transcript is sufficient for procedural questions about cross-entropy training, teacher forcing, corpus chunking, and backpropagation through time. No separate clip is needed.

question_bank.question: What loss is used to train the RNN language model at each time step?
choices: Mean squared error on the hidden state
Cross-entropy, equivalently negative log-likelihood of the actual next word
A hinge loss over all vocabulary items
Only an L2 penalty on the weights
question_bank.explanation: The model predicts a next-word distribution, and training penalizes how little probability it assigns to the true next token through cross-entropy or negative log-likelihood.

question_bank.question: What does teacher forcing do during training?
choices: It lets the model freely sample its own outputs at every step
It feeds the correct next token back in as the next input
It removes the output softmax during early epochs
It averages hidden states across the entire batch before prediction
question_bank.explanation: Teacher forcing uses the ground-truth token sequence during training so each prediction is conditioned on a real prefix rather than on the model’s sampled mistakes.

question_bank.question: Why are long corpora usually cut into manageable chunks for RNN training?
choices: Because softmax cannot be computed on long sequences
Because batching fixed-length segments is more convenient and efficient on GPUs
Because language models only work on paragraphs
Because backpropagation cannot handle repeated weights
question_bank.explanation: Long corpora are segmented so training can be vectorized and batched efficiently, rather than running one enormous sequence through the model at once.

question_bank.question: How is the gradient for a recurrent weight matrix computed across time?
choices: Only the final time step contributes to it
The gradients from every time step where it is used are summed
The largest gradient is kept and the rest are discarded
It is copied from the word-embedding matrix
question_bank.explanation: Because the same recurrent parameter appears at many time steps, its total gradient is the sum of the contributions from each occurrence.

transcript_quotes: each time step where it appears

question_bank.question: What is the main practical idea of truncated backpropagation through time?
choices: Shorten the backward pass to fewer steps while keeping forward processing practical
Replace the recurrent weights with convolutional filters
Train only on the first sentence of each document
Use sampled outputs instead of teacher forcing
question_bank.explanation: Truncated BPTT reduces training cost by limiting how far gradients are propagated backward, even though the model may still process longer forward contexts.
